body_txt: Legitimate talent agents do an important job in getting creative work out there. However, fiction tends to portray agents almost exclusively as scummy , self-important , uncreative , useless leeches who deliberately steal money from their clients. At best, they'll be bores who don't "understand" the creative process; at worst, they'll be immoral active criminals. They'll frequently be The Svengali , the Mean Boss , and the Corrupt Corporate Executive . Occasionally there will be a middle example who is Affably Evil but does do a genuinely good job for their clients, which will usually be more of a Punch-Clock Villain . Sometimes a Stage Mom will take things to a professional level by becoming one of these. Due to the prevalence of Write What You Know , most amoral agents will represent literature or actors. (Acting agents will usually deploy the Casting Couch .) However, it is at least possible for fictional agents to represent any and all sorts of work; these are just the most common. Compare Smooth-Talking Talent Agent , which may be an evil one but isn't inherently evil. The "evil" or bad agent trope usually draws from a combination of Amoral Attorney (with which it may overlap, given that some agents have legal training), Inhuman Resources , Horrible Hollywood , and Industrialized Evil . Contrast Da Editor , which is usually a heroic alternative for writers. Though this can be Truth in Television , no real-life examples, please!
